title: Google Lighthouse Introduces Experimental 'Agentic Navigation Score' — Is Your Site Ready for AI Agents?
content:
Google has quietly rolled out a groundbreaking experimental category inside Lighthouse: the Agentic Navigation Score. Already visible in PageSpeed Insights for sites running Chrome 150+, this new metric evaluates how well a website is structured for autonomous AI agent interaction — not just human visitors.

Unlike traditional 0–100 performance scores, the Agentic Navigation Score uses a binary checklist format (e.g., 3/3), measuring three critical signals:

1. WebMCP Integration — Does the site expose tools to AI agents via the Web Model Context Protocol? This includes both imperative JavaScript registration (navigator.modelContext.registerTool()) and declarative HTML form annotations with toolname and tooldescription attributes.

2. Agent-Oriented Accessibility — Goes beyond traditional ARIA compliance. Lighthouse now checks whether the accessibility tree provides machine-readable programmatic names, valid roles, and clear parent-child relationships that allow AI agents to navigate DOM elements autonomously.

3. Stability & Discoverability — CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) is re-evaluated specifically for agent context: layout shifts during interaction can cause agents to click wrong targets. Additionally, Lighthouse checks for the presence of a llms.txt file for agent discoverability — despite Google Search confirming it doesn't use llms.txt for ranking, it remains critical for agentic browsing contexts.

This signals a major paradigm shift: AEO (Agent-Engine Optimization) is becoming parallel to SEO. Sites that implement WebMCP standards and optimize for both human and machine navigation will gain first-mover advantage as AI agent traffic — already trackable via GA4's 'AI Assistant' channel — continues to surge.

Expert Perspective: As an early implementer of the WebMCP standard, I can confirm that achieving a perfect agentic score requires rethinking your entire frontend architecture. Forms must be annotated for agent discovery, search must be exposed as callable tools, and layout stability must be bulletproof. This is no longer optional for forward-thinking e-commerce sites.
